# 

# How to Use This Fillable Template {#how-to-use-this-fillable-template}

## Overview {#overview}

This fillable template is meant to be used **alongside** the *Thrive* workbook. 

The branding section of *Thrive* is a key component of this course—because so many other parts of your business depends on it:

* Price—what prospective clients are willing to pay you depends on the perceived value of what you have to offer… and your brand is a key element in presenting your value  
* Market—your marketing will help you be seen by many, but your brand is what makes you memorable  
* Book—creating a personal connection is important when turning inquiries into bookings, and you can begin creating that emotional connection through your branding

When you take the time to create a unique-to-you, irreplicable brand, you simplify a couples’ decision-making process—because you’ll be the obvious, standout “yes.”

The exercises throughout this section of *Thrive* will help you craft a unique brand that irresistibly attracts your perfect-fit couples, and they are so important I didn’t ever want you to feel limited by how much you could write down or discover—which is why this fillable template exists for the “Brand” section of the course. 

Now you can write down all of your thoughts without limitations and always have a place to write down future notes about your branding.

Let’s get into it,  
Maddie Mae

## Notes {#notes}

Anytime you see a box like the one below, this is a space designed for you to capture all of your thoughts:

| *Your ideas go here\!* |
| :---- |

# Brand Core \- Your Mission, Vision, & Values {#brand-core---your-mission,-vision,-&-values}

## Create Your Vision Statement {#create-your-vision-statement}

**What is a vision statement?**

A vision statement is *your* vision for where you see your business headed and why.

It’s your “big aspirational goal” for your business and describes what you want to accomplish and what impact you want to make. 

At its core, how you can fulfill your “why” with your photography business.

**Your “why” can inform your vision statement—so place your personal “why” statement that you drafted earlier in the course here:**

|  |
| :---- |

**Questions and prompts to help you brainstorm:**

* What impact do you feel personally motivated to make on this industry? What legacy do you want to leave behind?  
* If money were no object and anything was possible, what’s your vision for your business?  
* What kind of meaning and impact could your business have that would excite you, bring you joy, inspire you, and make you come alive?  
* What problems do you see couples face in their process of having their ideal wedding day that you feel the most motivated to solve?  
* In an ideal world, what do you think every couple should be able to experience on their wedding day—or would every wedding experience a couple would get to have?  
* Beyond giving couples beautiful images, what do you hope clients will say about their experience working with you and the impact your photography had on their lives?  
* Is there an “idealistic goal” that you go into every wedding or elopement or session trying to achieve?  
* How do you want your work to contribute to the larger narrative of a couple's love story or and their wedding experience?

* Complete the sentence:  
  * My vision for every couple I work with is…   
  * What I think the best thing every couple should be able to experience on their wedding day is…  
  * My ultimate dream for my business is…   
  * If anything was possible, my crazy big dream to accomplish with my business would be…. 

| ✧ Pro Tip: If you're struggling to figure out your impact, read through your client reviews.  |
| :---- |

**Now, using your “why” and the prompts above, draft your vision statement** 

*In one sentence* \- write down what your business aspires to be in the future including what you want to accomplish and what impact you hope to make.

What’s your “big aspirational goal” for your business?

**My vision is…** 

|  |
| :---- |

Remember, it’s just a brief description of where your business is headed. You don’t need to get into the “how” yet.

Don’t get stuck on it or worry about perfecting it. Just draft it and know it can change and evolve over time.

Ultimately, you are the only one who needs to feel good about your vision statement—it’s an internal statement to inspire and guide *you*, just like your why.

| ✧ Vision Statement Examples \- *to inspire you if you’re feeling stuck* Southwest Airlines: To become the world’s most loved, most flown, and most profitable airline.  Ikea: To create a better everyday life for many people.  Alzheimer's Association: A world without Alzheimer's and all other dementia. Teach for America: One day, all children in this nation will have the opportunity to attain an excellent education. Supergoop\!: Change the way the world thinks about sunscreen. Land O'Lakes: Our vision is to be one of the best food and agricultural companies in the world. Zappos: Our vision is delivering happiness to customers, employees and vendors. LinkedIn: To create economic opportunity for every member of the global workforce. Wikipedia: A world in which every single person is given free access to the sum of all human knowledge. |
| :---- |

## 

## Create Your Mission Statement {#create-your-mission-statement}

**What is a mission statement?**

A mission statement is essentially *how* you intend to achieve the vision you outlined in the previous section. 

It should be action-oriented and specifically address *how* you are going to achieve that “big aspirational goal” you set for yourself in your vision statement.

| ✧ Pro Tip: Your vision is the “why,” your mission is the “how”  |
| :---- |

**Questions and prompts to help you brainstorm:**

* There are often many ways to achieve the same vision, but how specifically are you going to do it with the business you have (or are creating)?  
* What specific key actions will it take to reach that “ideal future” (i.e. vision) you set for yourself?  
* In one sentence, summarize what your business does. Be sure to include:  
  * What you do  
  * How you do it   
  * Who you do it for  
  * Why it matters (what impact it makes)  
* What impact do you aim to contribute to each of your couple’s wedding experiences, and long term?   
* What quality and level of service do you provide? And how do you make that happen?  
* What kind of imagery do you create? How do you achieve it?   
* What impact do you aim for your business to have on the wider industry and the world? 

**Now, using your vision statement and the prompts above, draft your mission statement** 

*In one sentence* \- write down what your business does. Describe your business’ core purpose, including what it does, how it does it, who it does it for, and why it matters.

**My mission is…** 

|  |
| :---- |

Remember, *you* are the only one who needs to feel good about your mission statement. It’s an internal statement, designed to give *you* focus. 

Don’t get stuck on it or worry about perfecting it. Just draft it and know you will probably come back and keep refining it as you go through this course.

| ✧ Mission Statement Examples \- *to inspire you if you’re feeling stuck* Southwest Airlines: The mission of Southwest Airlines is dedication to the highest quality of customer service delivered with a sense of warmth, friendliness, individual pride, and company spirit. Ikea: Offer a wide range of well-designed, functional home furnishing products at prices so low that as many people as possible will be able to afford them. Patagonia: Build the best product, Cause no unnecessary harm, Use business to protect nature, Not bound by convention. Cradles to Crayons: Provides children from birth through age 12, living in homeless or low-income situations, with the essential items they need to thrive – at home, at school, and at play. Swarovski: Swarovski adds sparkle to everyday life with high-quality products and services that exceed our customers’ desires. We inspire our colleagues with innovation and reward their achievements while striving to expand our market leadership. Starbucks: With every cup, with every conversation, with every community—we nurture the limitless possibilities of human connection. Disney: To entertain, inform and inspire people around the globe through the power of unparalleled storytelling. Leave No Trace Center for Outdoor Ethics: Using the power of science, education and stewardship, Leave No Trace is on a mission to ensure a sustainable future for the outdoors and the planet. |
| :---- |

## 

## Identify Your Core Values {#identify-your-core-values}

**What are values?**

Your business’ values are the guiding principles that drive your mission forward by summarizing the most important ideals and ethics your business always upholds—even if times are tough or if you are at risk of losing money. Your non-negotiable core values factor into every professional decision you make, and they should respond to questions like “what principles does my business need to embody to get our mission done?” and “what ideals do I want the clients I am working with to share?” 

**Step 1: Highlight the core business values that resonate with you:**

\*Note: there does not need to be 5 groups

These are your core values, and they should align with your mission and vision statements.

| Group 1 | Group 2 | Group 3 | Group 4 | Group 5 |

# 

# Brand Positioning \- The Power of Specializing & Types of Specialties {#brand-positioning---the-power-of-specializing-&-types-of-specialties}

## Start to Brainstorm Your Specialty {#start-to-brainstorm-your-specialty}

**What can you specialize in?**

There are so many options for what you can specialize in—which is why the following section is going to give you recommendations for different categories you can consider for your unique speciality.

**Your “why” can inform your speciality as well—so once again place your personal “why” statement from earlier in the course here:**

|  |
| :---- |

**Step 1: Read through the below options and highlight any that catch your attention—or that you would be interested in offering:**

| Category | Examples |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Certain Size of Event** | Big traditional weddings Small weddings Intimate weddings Micro weddings Elopements |
| **Location of Wedding** *(specific geographic area)* | Be the trusted go-to local expert for: A city *(San Francisco, New York City, Paris, Copenhagen, Los Angeles, Tokyo, Kansas City, Toronto, São Paulo, Shanghai, Seoul, London, Mexico City)* A National Park *(Glacier National Park, Banff National Park, Northeast Greenland National Park, Wrangell-St Elias National Park, Namib-Naukluft National Park)* A region *(Moab, Blue Ridge Mountains, Pacific Northwest)* State *(Ohio, New Mexico, Illinois, Maryland, Iowa, Nebraska, Delaware)* A country *(Iceland, Barbados, Australia, Philippines, Germany, Mexico, Poland, India, Italy)* A certain climate/geography *(mountain weddings, beach elopements, forest micro weddings, desert elopements)* A type of location *(big cities, remote wilderness, national parks, national forests, city parks)* A type of venue *(vineyards, barns, backyard, house of worship, ski lodges, castles, museums, high-end resorts, historic sites, botanical gardens, treehouses, boats, breweries, planetariums, theme parks)* A specific venue with enough weddings or elopements to sustain you *(a city hall if you live in a big city, a specific Las Vegas chapel)* |
| **Style of Wedding Day** | Adventurous Alternative Antique Art Deco Botanical Celestial Chic Classic Colorful Contemporary Countryside Cowboy Eclectic Elegant Extravagant Fairytale Fantasy Festival Floral Glamorous Gothic Grecian Industrial Maximalist Minimalist Modern Movie- or book-inspired Nautical Pastel Pop art-inspired Preppy Quirky Regency-era Renaissance Retro Rustic Seasonal Steampunk Tropical Vintage Whimsical |
| **Type of Couple** *(based on the value(s) they prioritize or the unique challenges they face)* | Couples planning an eco-friendly wedding Couples coordinating a family-centered wedding Couples with restrictions due to their jobs, like being stationed with the military Couples with specific religious or cultural norms that are significant to them Couples traveling to your local area (if you live in a location with destination weddings or elopements) Couples struggling to figure out where in the world they want to elope Couples having a friends-only intimate weddings |
| **Specific Wedding Day Activity/Activities** | 4x4 off-roading Acrobatics Amusement park receptions Arcade games Backpacking Backyard games Board games Camping Candlelit ceremonies Canoeing Choreographed dancing Cliff-jumping Enjoying art Escape room Getting ready together Getting tattoos Glamping Green screen Helicopter Hiking Hot air balloon ride Ice skating Jet skis Live music Morning yoga Paddleboarding Painting Riding bikes Rock climbing Sailboat Scavenger hunt Skiing Skydiving Snowmobiling Spending time with pets or animals Stargazing Trivia Watching a live performance Wine tasting |
| **Style of Photography** | Aerial Airy Black and white Cinematic Classic Colorful Desaturated Documentary Dramatic Editorial Film Fine art Hyperstylized Landscape Lifestyle Moody Photojournalistic Retouched perfection True-to-color Underwater Vintage Warm |
| **Specific Technique or Method** | Posing style you created that nobody else uses A unique component of your planning process that no other photographer includes In-person consultations for potential clients local to you A completely different workflow from other photographers that benefit your clients in a different way Monthly planning consultation calls if you’re working with thorough, specific clients who would appreciate those A unique deliverable that other photographers don’t offer Allowing your clients to choose between different editing styles Elements of fun personalization throughout your workflow |
| **Unique Offering, Service, Package, or Experience** | Photography & videography Planning & photography Photography & officiating Photography & activity-guiding (with certification) Photography & partnering with a unique local venue Photography & floral design Photography & custom wedding invitation suite designs Photography & stylized elopement ceremony set-up in locations that allow it Photographer teams All-inclusive elopement packages Surprise elopement packages Surprise day-after activities photographed by you A day-after session specific to your area or niche (exploring hidden gem spots in New York City if you are a local there—or partnering with a nearby paragliding company to photograph a unique experience) Film photography or videography (i.e. Super 8\) Providing social media assets from your second photographer Client guides personalized to your couples’ wedding days |

**Step 2: Set a timer for 10 minutes and try to brainstorm some specialities of your own**

You don’t have to come up with options in each category—it’s totally fine if you spend the entire time writing down ideas for “style of wedding day.” 

All that matters is really trying to hone in on what is unique, important, and authentic to *you* (because that’s what will set you apart from the competition)\!

| Certain Size of Event |  |
| ----: | :---- |
| **Location of Wedding** *(specific geographic area)* |  |
| **Style of Wedding Day** |  |
| **Type of Couple** *(based on value(s) or challenges)* |  |
| **Specific Wedding Day Activity/Activities** |  |
| **Style of Photography** |  |
| **Specific Technique or Method** |  |
| **Unique Offering, Service, Package, or Experience** |  |

**Step 3: Reflect**

**Are there any specific specialties—or combinations—that stand out to you right now?** *(Don’t worry—you’re not making any finalized decisions yet\! This is just a space so you can jot down anything that’s currently standing out to you… then the next video will help you narrow down from here.)*

|  |
| :---- |

# 

# Brand Positioning \- Define Your Speciality  {#brand-positioning---define-your-speciality}

## High’s & Low’s \- 4 Lists {#high’s-&-low’s---4-lists}

Thinking back to past sessions and couples you’ve photographed is a great way to use your real experiences to explore commonalities and identify themes—and to uncover insights about how you want to position your photography business.

It is helpful to have at least one season of experience to reflect on, and honestly as much data as you can… 

But even if you are brand new, you can still do this exercise—consider your experiences second-shooting, assisting, attending loved ones’ weddings & elopements, people you’ve enjoyed working with in past jobs, and who you collaborated well with at school\!

| ✧ Pro Tip: If you are new and don’t have a lot of super relevant experience to draw on right now, set a reminder to revisit this exercise a year from now\! |
| :---- |

### List \#1: Describe Your MOST Fulfilling Sessions {#list-#1:-describe-your-most-fulfilling-sessions}

In this section, describe your best, favorite, most fulfilling weddings, elopements, sessions, or photoshoots that you’ve ever photographed. Do at least 4, but more is better—if you’ve been photographing for a full season, I’d recommend at least 6\. Copy and paste as needed\!

Don’t worry about *who* you were photographing yet (that comes next\!)—but rather ***what*** the event was and why you found it fulfilling to photograph. 

Write down as many details as you can about:

- The location—climate, geography, scenery, type of venue  
- Size of the event  
- Style/vibe/decor  
- Experience  
- Activities

… and so on. Use descriptive words and adjectives\! 

**Access your [Past Client Data Tracker for the four lists exercise here](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/17zKQCSV5RLslMZ8B1ntuE8Huz3N4EJLQIaVZQbdlhDw/copy?usp=sharing)**

| ✧ Pro Tip: If you haven’t been to an event yet or had a session that checked all of your boxes, write down what you *did* like—and you can piece together different parts of sessions to get a more complete picture of what you enjoy photographing the most. |
| :---- |

### List \#2: Describe Your Favorite Clients That Made You Feel the MOST Valued {#list-#2:-describe-your-favorite-clients-that-made-you-feel-the-most-valued}

In this section, describe your best, favorite clients that made you feel valued, fulfilled, and excited—who you connected with the deepest and really stand out in your mind. I’d recommend doing all 6 if you can\!

Once again, write down as many details as you can—their qualities and personalities, which includes:

- demographics  
- behaviors  
- personal style/vibe  
- values, beliefs, and motivators  
- why they connected with you

Try to find as much information as you can—and skip any sections you can’t find the answers to.

Not sure where to find this information? I’d recommend looking at:

- Your clients’ social media accounts (Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, Twitter, etc.)  
- Your clients’ LinkedIn  
- All past communications with your clients  
  - Initial inquiry  
  - Questionnaire responses  
  - Emails  
  - Text messages  
- The review your client(s) left you

Or consider trying to reconnect with them\!

This will be the most time-consuming part of these exercises, but I promise you it will be absolutely worth it\!

**Access your [Past Client Data Tracker for the four lists exercise here](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/17zKQCSV5RLslMZ8B1ntuE8Huz3N4EJLQIaVZQbdlhDw/copy?usp=sharing)**

| ✧ Pro Tip: Remember sometimes the people you feel most fulfilled by will coincide with the type of events they have (the *what* described in the previous list)... but things can go astray, so sometimes your favorite clients didn’t necessarily have a session you really loved. Really focus on the *who* here\! |
| :---- |

### 

### 

### List \#3: Describe Your LEAST Fulfilling Sessions {#list-#3:-describe-your-least-fulfilling-sessions}

In this section, describe your least favorite, least fulfilling weddings, elopements, sessions, or photoshoots that you’ve ever photographed—the ones that stand out to you because they just weren’t a good fit.

Don’t worry about *who* you were photographing (the clients planning these sessions may have even been some of your favorites that you have worked with)… instead, write down ***what*** the event was and why it wasn’t a good fit for you personally.

Write down what you can remember about:

- The location—climate, geography, scenery, type of venue  
- Size of the event  
- Style/vibe/decor  
- Timing  
- Experience  
- Activities

Be as descriptive as you can—marketing is all about *attracting* and *repelling*—and this exercise will help you figure out the latter. 

**Access your [Past Client Data Tracker for the four lists exercise here](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/17zKQCSV5RLslMZ8B1ntuE8Huz3N4EJLQIaVZQbdlhDw/copy?usp=sharing)**

| ✧ Pro Tip: It’s okay to be honest here. These are your *personal preferences,* and remember the sessions that might have been the “worst” for you might also be the *best* for someone else. |
| :---- |

### 

### List \#4: Describe the Clients That Made You Feel the LEAST Valued {#list-#4:-describe-the-clients-that-made-you-feel-the-least-valued}

In this section, describe the clients that made you feel the least valued, least fulfilled, least excited, or least effective—the ones who likely stand out in your mind due to a lack of compatibility or connection. This can help you start to understand *why* they weren’t a good personal match with you.

Unlike List \#2, you don’t have to go deep into the research here—just write down the information you remember and can easily access, including:

- demographics  
- behaviors  
- personal style/vibe  
- values, beliefs, and motivators  
- why they didn’t connect well or feel compatible with you

Doing this exercise can sometimes help us identify the more subtle qualities of our best-fit clients that we might not have identified without the contrast.

**Access your [Past Client Data Tracker for the four lists exercise here](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/17zKQCSV5RLslMZ8B1ntuE8Huz3N4EJLQIaVZQbdlhDw/copy?usp=sharing)**

| ✧ Pro Tip: This isn’t you passing judgment on them—this is about a lack of compatibility and connection. Remember, your service doesn’t need to be for everyone. Once you’ve identified the couples that you are not the best photographer to help them, then they can continue looking for the photographer who is the best fit for them—it benefits you both. |
| :---- |

### 

### 

### Identifying Themes and Patterns From Your 4 Lists {#identifying-themes-and-patterns-from-your-4-lists}

You’ve done all of the hard work\! Now it is time to take all of this information and summarize it in a way that is incredibly helpful for you. 

Go back through each of your 4 lists and highlight elements that come up more than once—this could be commonalities in the size of events, certain locations, activities, types of couples, values, etc.

Once you’ve identified these similarities, come back to this section and add them here.

| Similarities from List \#1: Describe Your MOST Fulfilling Sessions |
| :---- |
|  |

| Similarities from List \#2: Describe Your Favorite Clients That Made You Feel the MOST Valued |
| :---- |
|  |

| Similarities from List \#3: Describe Your LEAST Fulfilling Sessions |
| :---- |
|  |

| Similarities from List \#4: Describe the Clients That Made You Feel the LEAST Valued |
| :---- |
|  |

## Discover Your Differentiators {#discover-your-differentiators}

Now you are going to explore what makes you uniquely qualified for your specialty by considering the characteristics and elements of your life that are a part of who you are—and also difficult to replicate by anyone else. 

**Questions and prompts to help you brainstorm:**

* Where are you located?  
  * What’s unique about where you’re located?   
  * What knowledge do you have as a result of where you are located that others based elsewhere might not know?  
* Who do you work with?  
  * Is it just you or do you work with someone else (or could you)?  
  * What would that collaboration make you uniquely qualified for or to do?  
* What’s unique about you?  
  * What unique values, identity, strengths, skills, and talents do you have?  
  * What words or phrases would you use to describe your personality or identity?  
  * What do you care about more than others—your motivators, values, and passions?  
  * What hobbies or interests do you have outside of photography—and could you include them in your business somehow?  
  * What unusual opinions do you have that might resonate with your target market?   
  * Do you have specific certifications or credentials that others do not?  
  * What past experience do you have that you could or do draw upon?  
  * What education have you received?  
  * What accomplishments have you been recognized for?  
  * What uncommon life experiences have you had that impacted who you are or what you are capable of?  
  * How do you approach problems differently than others?  
* What do you enjoy that others don’t—especially in the photography process?  
  * Is there a specific part of the process you do or could help with that others do not?  
  * Do you include steps in your workflow that you would guess others do not?  
* What do you have that not every other photographer does? *(Don’t worry—it doesn’t have to be 100% unique-to-you… just something that not 100% of photographers have, offer, or do.)*  
  * Can you weave together several elements into a unique service that others can’t?  
* What do your clients say you do that stands out or matters to them?  
  * Revisit your reviews—what is the exact wording they use when talking about what was great about working with you?  
  * What has positively surprised them about working with you?  
  * Do you interact with clients in a notable way?   
* Do you have unique access to something others don’t? For example, unique access to…  
  * A specific relationship *(i.e. a professional vendor)*?  
  * Helpful connections *(i.e. your personal life or network)*?  
  * A useful resource *(i.e. an activity guide)*?  
  * A location that others don’t *(i.e. being a preferred vendor for a venue that requires all clients to work with preferred vendors)*?  
  * Unusual technology or equipment  *(i.e. a hard-to-find piece of gear or a camera body that is currently unavailable for purchase because it is sold out)*?

**Now list out everything you can think of that could be potential differentiators:**

|  |
| :---- |

## 

## Draft Your Specialty Statement {#draft-your-specialty-statement}

**What is a specialty statement?**

A specialty statement is a summary of who you are, what you do, who you do it for, and why it matters. You won’t be sharing it anywhere—it is an internal statement for you to determine how to concisely and meaningfully communicate your unique value. 

| ✧ Pro Tip: This is also great time to revisit and refine your mission statement from earlier\!  |
| :---- |

**Specialty statement option \#1**

**I specialize in** \[location/type/style of wedding/elopement\] **for** \[describe couple–values/unique challenges\] **who want** \[style of photography/type of service/unique offer/emotional quality of wedding day\] **because I** \[3 unique things about you that not every other photographer can say about themselves\].

**Example: *I specialize in*** *Portland elopement photographer **for** alternative couples **who want** to immerse themselves in the culture of the city on their wedding day and have it documented with colorful photography **because I** was born and raised in Portland, know the city better than I know myself most days, and have carefully curated a bold, colorful style that stands out.*

I specialize in \[                                                                  \] for \[                                                                \] who want \[                                                                                                                                      \] because I:  
\[1\)                                                                                               .                                                        
 2\)                                                                                              .   
 3\)                                                                                               \].

**Specialty statement option \#2**

**I am a** \[location/type/style\] \[wedding/elopement\] **photographer who offers** \[style of photography/type of service/unique offer/emotional quality of wedding day\]**—based on** \[3 unique things about you that not every other photographer can say about themselves\]**—for** \[describe couple–values/unique challenges\] **because** \[why it matters\].

**Example: *I am a*** *fantasy wedding **photographer who offers** a truly once-in-a-lifetime magical experience**—based on** my experience with live action role-playing (LARPing), my lifelong love of reading fantasy, and my thoroughly developed Photoshop skills**—for** couples who yearn for something more enchanted than reality **because** I believe your wedding day should feel otherworldly.*

I am a \[                                                                  \] \[                                                                \] photographer who offers \[                                                                                                                    \]— based on \[1\)                                                                                              \],   
\[ 2\)                                                                                               \], and   
\[ 3\)                                                                                               \]—  
for \[                                                                \] because  \[                                                                \].

**Specialty statement option \#3**

\[3 unique things about you that not every other photographer can say about themselves\] **allow me to specialize in** \[location/type/style\] \[wedding/elopement\] **for** \[describe couple–values/unique challenges\]**—and I accomplish this through** \[style of photography/type of service/unique offer/emotional quality of wedding day\].

**Example:** *My MFA (Masters of Fine Arts), experience in fashion photography, and highly-stylized posing method **allow me to specialize in** upscale urban weddings and elopements **for** couples who value artistry and often work in the fashion industry—**and I accomplish this through** a highly-personalized and thorough workflow, as well as a very practiced editorial style of photography.*

\[1\)                                                                                               .                                                        
 2\)                                                                                              .   
 3\)                                                                                               \] allow me to specialize in  
\[                                                                  \] for \[                                                                \]—and I accomplish this through \[                                                                                                                        \].

**Place your specialty statement here:**

|  |
| :---- |

# Brand Positioning \- Identify your Target Market {#brand-positioning---identify-your-target-market}

## Define Your Target Market {#define-your-target-market}

**What is a target market?**

Your target market is made of your best fit and most likely potential clients. It is a defined segment of a larger market (all engaged individuals planning a wedding and looking to hire a photographer) and is made up of individuals with shared qualities and characteristics that make up the types of couples you love to work with and are likely to be planning the types of wedding days you want to photograph.

In this exercise, you’ll be looking at your target market’s:

- demographics  
- geographics  
- behaviors  
- Psychographics

![][image2]

**Step 1: Fill out the below information about your target market:**

The good news: you actually started finding a lot of this data during the “High’s & Low’s \- 4 Lists” exercise.   Now that you have collected, reviewed, and expanded the information you have about your favorite clients and sessions, you are now ready to generalize this information to thoroughly define your target market.

| Demographics |  |
| ----- | :---- |
| Age Range |  |
| Education |  |
| Occupation |  |
| Income |  |
| Marital Status *(if engaged, how long have they been together for?)* |  |
| Other demographic information |  |
| **Geographics** |  |
| Cities |  |
| States |  |
| Countries |  |
| Regions *(i.e. big cities, small cities, rural)* |  |
| Environments *(i.e. city, suburbia, desert, forest)* |  |
| Are they local to where they are getting married? *(also consider if they’ve been to their wedding location before)* |  |
| Other geographic information |  |
| **Behaviors** |  |
| How they act |  |
| What they like to do |  |
| What they do not like to do |  |
| What they do to relax |  |
| Hobbies |  |
| Interests |  |
| How they socialize |  |
| How they spend their time |  |
| How they spend their money |  |
| Where they shop |  |
| Brands they support or enjoy |  |
| Sources of information |  |
| Social media |  |
| Other behavior information |  |
| **Psychographics** *(especially related to getting married)* |  |
| Values |  |
| Motivators |  |
| Goals |  |
| Desires *(think about how many people will be attending)* |  |
| Challenges *(think about when & where they want to get married & what questions they might have)* |  |
| Fears |  |
| Lifestyle |  |
| Other psychographic information *(i.e. how far they are into the planning process when they reach out to you)* |  |
| **Additional Notes** |  |
|  |  |

| ✧ Pro Tip: If you are struggling to identify the motivators, goals, desires, challenges, and fears of couples planning elopements, I would recommend reading through the [“Reasons to Elope From 200+ Real Couples”](https://adventureinstead.com/top-reasons-to-elope/) blog post I compiled—it is based on a survey that over 200 couples who eloped responded to\! |
| :---- |

## 

## Profiling Your Ideal Client Avatars (ICAs) {#profiling-your-ideal-client-avatars-(icas)}

**What is an ideal client avatar (ICA)?**

An ideal client avatar is an individual—or, in our case, couple—in your target market. Your target market is made up of many ICAs, but we’re just going to make a few to help you really imagine your marketing and content from your couples’ perspectives.

ICAs can be incredibly useful when creating marketing content because it can be intimidating to think about speaking to your entire target market all at once… this can help you imagine a very specific person (or couple) to talk to. 

**How many ICAs do you need?** 

Most wedding and elopement photographers only need 2-3. 

- If you photograph weddings **and** elopements, create one for each  
- If you are based in a **destination area**, create one for local couples and another for couples traveling in  
- If you have a **broader** speciality with several **different categories** of couples, consider doing a third

| ✧ Pro Tip: If diversity and inclusion are important to you, that starts *here* with who you are imagining your target market as and the ideal client avatars you choose to be representative of your target market.  |
| :---- |

### 

### First Ideal Client Avatar {#first-ideal-client-avatar}

**Description \- “Who”**

| Names & pronouns: |  |  | ![][image3] |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | ----- |
| Ages: |  | Occupations: |  |
| Where do they live? |  | Level of education: |  |
| What’s it like where they live? *(urban, rural, climate, seasons)* |  | Estimated combined annual income: |  |
| How long have they been together? What relationship milestones have they passed? |  | Cultural or religious identities & family background: |  |

**Behavior & Personality \- “What”**

| Hobbies & Interests:  What do they like to do?  How do they like to spend their time? How do they socialize? What events will they be attending? What communities are they a part of? |
| :---- |
|  |

| What do they choose to spend their money on?  What kinds of brands are they drawn to & why? (Think: Cars, clothing, where they shop for food, where they eat out, how they travel, what would be their dream vacation?) Where would they love to go? |
| :---- |
|  |

| What sources of information do they use & trust? Where do they look to get ideas, inspiration, get recommendations, and to learn things? Do they ask others? Google? Use Youtube? How do they use social media?  What websites do they go to? What blogs, books, and magazines might they read? |
| :---- |
|  |

| What’s their personal style & vibe (attire, home decor, etc.) |
| :---- |
|  |

**Motivators \- “Why”**

| What are their goals, desires, & values when it comes to how they want to get married?  What are they looking for out of their experience & photos?  Where do they want it to be & why?  Who do they want to be there & why? What are their biggest priorities? What do they want their wedding day to mean? What do they want their family & friends to be thinking & saying about how they got married, or about their wedding photos?  What are their core values? What matters a lot to them (that you can tell based on their life choices, career choices, purchasing habits, and how they want to get married)? What similarities are there to your values? |
| :---- |
| *Ex. They want… Their biggest priorities are…  Their values include a commitment to…  At the end of the day, what matters most to them is…*  |

| What are challenges, hurdles, and pain points with regards to having the wedding day & images they want? What are they looking to avoid when it comes to their wedding day or images? What are going to be the toughest parts of achieving the day and images they want? What could try to get in the way of them achieving their goals? Is there a “villain” in their story? What external problems are they facing? What challenges do they have separately from each other, if any? What are they struggling with outside of how they are getting married? |
| :---- |
| *Ex. They want to avoid…  The thing that could really get in their way is…*  |

| What are their fears, worries, and internal struggles throughout this process? How are the challenges and hurdles they’re facing making them feel? What’s stressing them out or frustrating them? Do they have self-doubts? About what? What stress or pressures could their friends or family be putting on them that they’re worried about? Because they don’t have the expertise you do, how could this all be making them feel? What internal or philosophical problems are they facing?  |
| :---- |
| *Ex. They’re frustrated with…  They’re afraid of…*  |

| What are their possible objections? What are the reasons they wouldn’t hire you? What are their fears about committing to booking a photographer? What are their burning questions about the process of booking a photographer and getting married? |
| :---- |
| *Ex.  They’re worried about…  They’re confused about…  They have a misconception that…*  |
