# 

# Brand Positioning \- Unique Value Proposition {#brand-positioning---unique-value-proposition}

**What is a unique value proposition?**

A unique value proposition (UVP) is an externally-facing statement of what you are going to do for your target market and what differentiates you from your competition—all in a single sentence. 

Some version of it will probably end up at the top of your homepage.

The 3 goals of every unique value proposition:

- express a **clear, compelling benefit** to your ideal client avatar  
- be **real, authentic, and relevant** to your ideal client avatar  
- **a promise** you can keep with every couple

| ✧ Unique Value Proposition Examples \- *to inspire you if you’re feeling stuck* FedEx: Your package will get there overnight. Guaranteed.  McDonald’s: An inexpensive, familiar & consistent meal delivered in a clean environment. Disney: Creating happiness through magical experiences. Patagonia: We provide for environmentally responsible adventure.  Yeti: Deliver exceptional performance and durability in any environment. LG: State-of-the-art Living Experience.  Samsung: Get Ready to Unfold Your World. Imperfect Foods: Groceries that help you fight food waste. Hulu: All The TV You Love. |
| :---- |

## Value Proposition Canvas {#value-proposition-canvas}

**What is a value proposition canvas?** 

A value proposition canvas is a summary of:

- **your ideal clients’…**  
  - **wants** (emotional drivers)  
  - **needs** (rational motivators)  
  - **fears** (undesired outcomes)  
- **your offer…**  
  - **differentiators** (what sets you apart)  
  - **product & service offerings** (think beyond photos, prints, and albums—what style of photos? What methods, approaches, and techniques will you be using?)  
  - **other value offerings** (what additional value are you providing them? What client experience do you create? What will you do for them or to help them with the planning process? What overall experience do you want them to have with you?)

![][image4]

| Your Offer |  | Your Ideal Client Avatar(s) |  |
| ----- | :---- | ----- | :---- |
| Differentiators |  | Wants |  |
| Product & Service Offerings |  | Needs |  |
| Other Value Offerings |  | Fears |  |

Once you’ve completed your value proposition canvas, highlight or underline anything that is mentioned on both the right (Your Ideal Client Avatar) and left sides (Your Offer). 

## 

## Select Your Top 3 Unique Differentiators {#select-your-top-3-unique-differentiators}

Now that you have listed out the different components of your offer and matched them to the wants, needs, and fears of your ideal client avatars, it is time to **select your top 3 unique differentiators**.

| Differentiator 1 | Differentiator 2 | Differentiator 3 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
|  |  |  |

| ✧ Pro Tip: Feeling stuck? Let’s take a moment to revisit the full list of differentiators you came up with during the earlier “Discover Your Differentiators” section. Are there any you missed on your Value Proposition Canvas that match up with the wants, needs, and fears of your ideal client avatars?  |
| :---- |

## 

## Write Your Unique Value Proposition {#write-your-unique-value-proposition}

There are many different templates you can use to help you draft your unique value proposition. Steve Blank’s value proposition template is the one demonstrated in the accompanying video—but you can use whichever one(s) work best for you\!

**Steve Blank’s Value Proposition Template**

**I help** \[target market\] **do** \[achieve goals/avoid challenges\] **by doing** \[the unique things we do & value we provide to our couples\]

**Example:** *I help couples seeking hidden, unique experiences in the city that never sleeps by sharing my insider knowledge to create their fantasy, intimate elopement in New York City and have it documented with bold & colorful imagery*

I help \[                                                                  \] do \[                                                                \] by doing \[                                                                  \]

**Geoff Moore Value Proposition Template**

**For** \[target market\] **who** \[needs or wants goal/desire\], \[company or offer name\] **is** \[industry or service\] **that** \[unique value\]

**Example:** *For couples who are planning joyful, family-focused backyard weddings, I am a photographer with a background in photojournalism who knows how to capture those candid moments so you can focus on what’s important—spending quality time with your loved ones.* 

For \[                                                                  \] who \[                                                                \],   
\[                                                                  \] is \[                                                                  \] that   
\[                                                                  \] 

**Cooper & Vlaskovits’ Value Proposition Template**

\[Target market\] \[problem\] \[solution\]

**Example:** *Couples wanting to plan an authentic, unique elopement unlike any other wedding day in the world might not know what’s been done before… I am an innovative elopement photographer with five years of experience who will skip past suggesting the obvious to help you plan a highly customized, irreplicable, and memorable experience that is entirely yours*

\[                                                                  \] \[                                                                \]  
\[                                                                  \]

**Eric Sink’s Value Proposition Template** 

**The** \[superlative or adjective\] \[offer\] **for** \[target market\]

**Example:** *Eclectic Las-Vegas-based elopement photography for spectacular, spontaneous couples traveling in to get married in the city of neon lights*

The \[                                                                  \] \[                                                                \]   
for \[                                                                  \]

**Dave McClure’s Value Proposition Template**

**I** \[what\] \[how\] \[why\]

**Example:** *I help you share every moment from your elopement with loved ones near and far by combining digital photography with social media content creation… so you can share Instagram-ready photos the day after your wedding—without having to break the news to your long-distance grandma it could take longer than a week for her to even see any photos from your wedding day*

I \[                                                                  \] \[                                                                \]   
\[                                                                  \]

**Simon Sinek’s Golden Circle as a Value Proposition Template**

“\[Why\] \[how\] \[what\].”

**Example:** *Sometimes the only family that deserves to be invited to your wedding is your found family. I approach photography and planning from the belief that family involvement should never be the default assumption—which is why I provide my couples with a friends-only wedding guide, recommend venues with on-site accommodations so you can spend as much uninterrupted time together, and capture every moment with documentary-style photography.*

\[                                                                  \] \[                                                                \]  
\[                                                                  \]

**Place the unique value proposition (UVP) you drafted here:** 

|  |
| :---- |

# 

# Brand Persona \- Brand Message and Story {#brand-persona---brand-message-and-story}

## Your Brand Story {#your-brand-story}

Donald Miller’s Story Brand framework is a clear, concise, and effective way to communicate who your business is, what you have to offer, and why it matters to prospective clients—in a way that is easily and quickly understood. 

Here are the seven elements:

* A Character  
* Has a Problem  
* And Meets a Guide  
* Who Gives Them a Plan  
* And Calls Them to Action  
* That Helps Them Avoid Failure  
* And Ends in a Success

### **A Character** {#a-character}

*“Everyone wants to be the hero of their own story.” \- Daniel Kahneman*

**WHO WANTS SOMETHING**

**Brainstorm a list of desires, needs, wants, and goals that your ideal client avatar(s) and target market of couples have:**

|  |  |
| :---- | ----- |

**Questions and prompts to help you brainstorm:**

* What do your couples (your specific target market/ICAs) want when it comes to their wedding/wedding photography?  
* What are their goals?   
* What do they desire most when it comes to their wedding experience?  
* What do they desire most when it comes to images of their wedding day?  
* What potential desires (related to survival) do your couples have when it comes to how they are getting married that you can help them achieve?

**Additional questions—based around common desires and Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs:**

![][image5]

* *Save time*  
  * What will be the most time-consuming steps of the planning process before your ICA’s wedding day?  
  * What will your ICA be spending more time than average on in their planning process?   
  * If your workflow is different from the average photographer’s, do any of those changes save your ICA time?  
* *Reduce waste*  
  * How much do your couples value “sustainability”?   
* *Love, intimacy, connection, community, belonging, and relationships*  
  * What kind of connection is your ICA looking for?  
  * What will make your ICA feel loved on their wedding day?  
* *Self-esteem and self-confidence*  
  * What does a wedding day that is “the most them” mean to your ICA?  
* *Aspirational identity and/or gaining status in the eyes of others*  
  * Is there a “better version” of themselves that your ICA is trying to become?  
  * Is your ICA trying to prove that they have a specific quality by how they are choosing to get married?  
  * What does this couple want their family & friends to be thinking & saying about how they got married—or about their wedding photos?   
  * How could working with us & getting the images we create help our ICA to be seen in their social circle how they want to be seen?  
* *Gaining something scarce—including accumulating resources*  
  * Look at your differentiators—what will a couple get by working with you that they won’t get from every other photographer? What do you offer that is the most “rare” that they would want?   
* *Find Meaning*  
  * What does a “meaningful wedding” day mean to your ICA?  
  * What kind of “meaning” are they searching for?  
* *Innate desire to be generous and/or aspirational identity of helping others*  
  * How important are having loved ones present to your ICA?   
  * If they are having guests present, what kind of experience does your ICA want to create for their guests?   
* *Self-actualization*  
  * What does the “best version” of your ICA look like?   
  * What does the “best version” of their wedding day look like?

**Now, narrow down to one core desire/need/want:**

* Which of these desires/goals stands out as the single most important one—the one that matters the most to your ICA and that you can promise couples will get by working with you?  
* You might like to phrase it using these templates:  
  * Couples who want \_\_\_\_\_\_\_      
  * Couples who love \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_  
  *  \_\_\_\_\_\_ couples  *(if it’s an aspirational identity)*

**My “character” (my couples) and their core desire is:**

|  |
| :---- |

### **Has a Problem** {#has-a-problem}

*“Companies typically sell solutions to external problems but people buy solutions to internal problems.” \- Donald Miller*

**VILLAIN**

In *Building a Story Brand,* Donald Miller establishes four characteristics of what a good villain should be:

* “**a root source.** Frustration, for example, is not a villain; frustration is what a villain makes us feel.” Try to find the source of strong negative emotions.  
* “**relatable**. When people hear us talk about the villain, they should immediately recognize it as something they disdain.”  
* “**singular**.” Having multiple villains when you are trying to construct an easily understood story can distract the focus.  
* “**Real**. Never go down the path of being a fearmonger. There are plenty of actual villains out there to fight.” 

The villain initiates external problems, which we will get into more with the next section.

**Brainstorm examples of “villains” that your couples face in their quest to having the wedding day they want:**

|  |
| :---- |

**Questions and prompts to help you brainstorm:**

* Is there something you see that’s *wrong* with the wedding industry that causes problems for the types of couples you want to help?  
  * What’s the root cause of that problem?  
  * Does it have a name? *(that could be a potential “villain” to name in their story)*  
* What external “evils” are your couples up against that you help them combat?  
* What do both you and your couples *hate* and want to *avoid*?  
* What does your brand stand *against*?   
* What are you & your couples “anti-”?  
  * Complete this sentence: *“Both me and my couples are anti- \_\_\_\_”*

**If you were able to come up with some possible “villains,” now narrow down to one:**

* Which stands out to you as the most “dastardly” villain of them all—the one your couples would be most motivated to conquer or avoid?

**The worst villain my couples face is:**

|  |
| :---- |

| ✧ Pro Tip: If you can’t think of any villains, then don’t overthink this—you brand story doesn't have to have a “villain” to be powerful. Just use it as a way to start brainstorming problems your couples face. |
| :---- |

**EXTERNAL PROBLEM**

External problems are the physical, tangible problems your ideal client avatars are experiencing. 

An wedding industry-specific example would be a couple interested in eloping in a place they’ve never been there before—and not knowing how to go about finding a potential private, beautiful place to say their vows.

**Brainstorm examples of external problems your business meets for couples. These are the common questions, issues, needs, pain points, challenges your couples face:**

|  |  |
| :---- | ----- |

**Questions and prompts to help you brainstorm:**

* What’s the most obvious need that your business meets for your couples?   
* My business solves couples’ need for \_\_\_\_\_\_\_  
* My business solves couples’ lack of \_\_\_\_\_\_\_  
* What’s most obviously at stake if someone doesn't hire you as their photographer for their wedding/elopement?   
* Couples won’t get  \_\_\_\_\_\_\_ if they don’t hire me  
* If couples don’t hire me, they would end up \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ (something negative/bad something they want to avoid)  
* Besides photos, what does a couple get out of hiring you? What need is that meeting?   
* For couples planning the type of weddings/elopements you specialize in, what are some of the most common, big-picture questions that would come up early on in their planning process? *(These are questions that they might Google or ask you in the inquiry-to-booking process—and can point to external problems they have… ex. Who, What, Where, When, How… types of questions)*  
* If you identified a villain in your brand story, what external problem is that villain causing?

| ✧ Pro Tip: Don’t worry if problems are accidentally placed in the wrong category (*villain, external, internal, philosophical)*—it’s more important that you are digging deep and identifying actual problems your target market face. |
| :---- |

**Now, narrow down to one most important external problem:**

* What external problem are your couples the most motivated to solve (which they solve by hiring you?) 

**The biggest external problem my couples face (that I help solve) is:**

|  |
| :---- |

**INTERNAL PROBLEM**

Internal problems are the difficult, negative, or frustrating emotions produced by external problems. 

**Brainstorm internal problems (frustrations, fears, or doubts) your coupes are feeling—that you can help solve with your service:**

|  |  |
| :---- | ----- |

**Questions and prompts to help you brainstorm:**

* How do the external problems your couples face make them feel inside? *(Remember: they don’t have the experience or expertise that you do).*   
* What is your ICA couple worried about?   
* What is stressing your ICA out?  
* What fears do they have?  
* What frustrations do they have?  
* What self-doubt issues do your couples face in the process of trying to have the wedding they want?  
* What are they self-conscious about?  
* What does your ICA couple want others to think about them?  
* What might your ICA couple’s family or friends be making them feel?  
* If your ICA couple was having doubts they were making the right decision about how they’re getting married, what would those doubts be?   
* What might your ICA couple be ashamed to even admit, but they are really questioning inside?   
* Deep down, what are they afraid of most?  
* What’s the “worst case scenario” they’re trying to avoid? 

**Now, narrow down to one most important internal problem:**

* Is there one that stands out as a theme that is a universal experience for your couples?  
* What is the “worst thing” that hurts the most or cuts the deepest?  
* What’s the stress, worry, or fear that would keep them up at night that they would be most motivated to solve?

**The worst internal problem my couples face (that I can help solve) is:**

|  |
| :---- |

| ✧ Pro Tip: make sure your brand story is *real* for your couples.  If the problems you talk about in your brand story aren’t *real* problems your target market of couples immediately recognize and identify with, those problems won’t resonate with them. So instead of “guessing,” make sure they are based on a true understanding of your target market—from research and observation. Here’s where you can look for & discover your target market’s real problems:  Go back and revisit the ways you can do research on your best fit couples in the target market video or the description for the “List \#2” exercise above. Then specifically search for desires (what your couples *say they want*) and problems (what they *say they don’t want*). Look for desires and problems in every question your best-fit couples have asked you in the inquiry-to-booking process, throughout their email exchanges, and their questionnaires Call any of your best-fit past clients and ask them about their experience of the process of getting married Hang out in a Facebook group full of your ICA couples *(i.e. a wedding planning or elopement planning group)* and scour Reddit threads—look at what couples *say they do want and don’t want* Add this question to your inquiry form and your questionnaire: why did you decide to have the wedding day you are having? And if you are photographing elopements: why did you decide to elope? This is a super powerful question I ask every couple who works with me Ask a couple why they chose this way… and they will probably tell you why they didn’t want it that way *(i.e. What was it about this type of wedding experience that made you choose it out of all the ways to get married?)* If you ask them why they chose this way, they will tell you it is because they wanted this and didn’t want that   These real problems are there, I promise. You CAN find them. |
| :---- |

**PHILOSOPHICAL PROBLEM**

**Brainstorm examples of philosophical problems your business meets for couples—which are problems are bigger than us, our couples, our businesses and problems that address why this matters on the grander scale of humanity:**

|  |  |
| :---- | ----- |

**Questions and prompts to help you brainstorm:**

* What is just plain wrong about the struggles your couples are facing?  
* Why is it unjust for your couples to face these struggles?  
* What ***ought*** or ***should*** every wedding day have, include, or be?  
* What ***shouldn’t*** every wedding day have, include, or be?  
* What myths, misconceptions, or lies are your couples believing about themselves or how they have to get married?  
  * What truth(s) do you want to share to push back against these myths, misconceptions, or lies?  
* Is there a philosophical wrong your brand stands against?  
* Is there something you see that’s wrong with the wedding industry as a whole that causes problems, fears, or stress for the types of couples you want to help?  
* Is there anything you addressed in your mission, vision, or values that could tie into a philosophical problem?   
* What issues do you feel passionate about and compelled to solve with your business?  
* What problems, stressors, anxieties, or concerns are they trying to escape from?  
* Is your brand part of a larger, more important story—and, if so, what is it?

| ✧ Pro Tip: Reference what you wrote down under “villain” for some ideas because sometimes there can be some overlap.  Ask yourself: *why is it unjust for people to have to suffer at the hands of this villain?* |
| :---- |

**Now, narrow down to one most relevant philosophical problem:**

* What philosophical problem is most deeply felt and most commonly struggled with by the couples in your target market?

**The biggest philosophical problem my couples face (that my business is positioned against) is:**

|  |
| :---- |

### **And Meets a Guide**

*“People are looking for a guide to help them, not another hero.” \- Donald Miller*

The guide is the role you will inhabit in your branding: the individual who comes alongside the hero—your client(s)—to guide them through their challenges and overcome their problems with *empathy* and *authority*…

And you will be communicating your differentiators so you’re not just any “guide”—you’ll be a uniquely qualified guide.

**EMPATHY**

Empathy is key because prospective clients won’t know how much you empathize or care about them if you don’t tell them… but it must be real—it can’t be faked or forced. 

**Brainstorm a list of ways you you empathize with your couples based on your personal experiences:**

|  |
| :---- |

**Questions and prompts to help you brainstorm:**

* What problems, frustrations, and fears do your ICAs have that you have experienced in the past?  
* What issues have you seen past couples struggle with (and possibly even helped them resolve) that your ICAs might also be dealing with?  
* What similar life experiences do you share with your ICAs?  
* What unique perspective do you have on the world that could improve the experience of your ICAs?  
* What demographics, geographics, behaviors, and psychographics do you share with your ICAs?  
  * Do any of these impact their wedding planning process or result in uncommon struggles?  
* If you’ve been in a relationship:  
  * What challenges have you and your partner overcome together that might be applicable?   
  * What emotions do you experience or have you experienced that you wish for your couple to feel on their wedding day?  
* If you’ve personally gotten married:  
  * What was the most difficult part of planning your wedding?  
  * What difficult, negative, or frustrating emotions did you feel leading up to your wedding day—and what caused those internal problems?  
  * What advice do you wish someone would have told you right after you got engaged?  
  * If you had to do it all over again, what would you have changed about the planning process leading up to your wedding day?  
  * What was the best part of your wedding day—and is there anything you can do as a couple’s photographer to help them experience that as well?  
* If you’ve helped a loved one plan their wedding day:  
  * What were the biggest problems you saw them encounter?  
  * What advice do you wish you could have been able to tell them?  
  * If you were to do it all over again, what would you do differently?

**Now that you have come up with a list of ways you empathize with your couples, take a moment to revisit your primary differentiators from the Unique Value Proposition section:** 

* Do any of the ways you empathize with your couples align with your differentiators?

**Now write 1-3 empathetic statements you can use to connect with your couples and demonstrate you understand what they are going through:**

|  |
| :---- |

| ✧ Pro Tip: Empathetic statements can start with the following phrases: “I understand how it feels to…” “I believe nobody should have to experience…” “I am also frustrated by…” “I care about…” “I know firsthand what it’s like to…” |
| :---- |

**AUTHORITY**

There are five ways to demonstrate authority:

1. Social proof—including testimonials and reviews  
2. Awards, features, and publications  
3. Statistics  
4. Qualifications and certifications  
5. Sharing knowledge education

**SOCIAL PROOF: If you have positive reviews from previous clients, take a few minutes to read through at least 3 of them—and, as you do so, copy and paste any mentions of when you helped them solve problems here:**

|  |
| :---- |

   
**AWARDS, FEATURES, AND PUBLICATIONS: If you have won any awards, received any features, or been published, make a list of those recognitions:**

|  |
| :---- |

| ✧ Pro Tip: Save the logos or badges along the way as you are doing this—these will be helpful for your website later if you don’t already have them there\! |
| :---- |

   
**STATISTICS: Brainstorm a list of relevant and compelling statistics, facts, and figures you could share:**

|  |
| :---- |

**Questions and prompts to help you brainstorm:**

* How many years of photography experience do you have?  
* How many couples have you helped?  
* How many photography sessions have you done?  
* How many five-star reviews do you have?  
* How many photos have you taken in your lifetime?  
* How many hours have you spent photographing since starting your business?  
* How many states have you photographed in?  
* Differentiator-specific statistics—for example, if you are specializing in…  
  * **Your local area:** How many years have you lived in the area for? How many local organizations have you collaborated or worked with? How many nearby landmarks have you visited or photographed at?  
  * **Destination weddings:** How many countries have you traveled to? Photographed in?  
  * **Hiking elopements:** How many miles have you hiked? How many of those miles have been with couples? How many states or countries have you hiked in? How many national parks have you been to and photographed in?  
  * **Family-oriented small weddings:** How many family sessions have you photographed? How many family members are in your average photo?  
  * **Fantasy intimate weddings:** How many Renaissance fairs have you been to? How many fantasy photography sessions have you done? If you deliver photos Photoshopped to include fantasy elements, how many do you include per package/hour of coverage?  
  * **Microweddings in museums:** How many museums have you visited worldwide? Photographed in? How many famous paintings have you recreated with your couples?

**QUALIFICATIONS AND CERTIFICATIONS: Are there any qualifications or certifications you have—especially recognized by your niche or speciality?** 

|  |
| :---- |

   
**SHARING KNOWLEDGE & EDUCATION:** One of the best ways to demonstrate expertise is to actually share education informed by it—and an easy and effective way to do this is through correcting misconceptions with truth and mythbusting. 

**Brainstorm some common myths and misconceptions that might be misleading your couples—and then counter them with a simple truth that resolves that problem for them:** 

| Myth or Misconception | The Simple Truth |
| ----- | ----- |
|  |  |

| ✧ Pro Tip: Two common formats are: You might be wondering *\[myth/misconception\]*... but the truth is *\[the simple truth\]* A common misconception is *\[myth/misconception\]*... but the fact of the matter is that *\[the simple truth\]* Still struggling to come up with myths or misconceptions? You might have come up with a few when you were brainstorming philosophical problems—so reference back to that section if you are feeling stuck here\! |
| :---- |

   
**BONUS: Do you have any uncommon expertise or authority based on your differentiators?** 

|  |
| :---- |

   
**Now write 1-3 statements that demonstrate your authority:** 

|  |
| :---- |

### **Who Gives Them a Plan**

*“Remember, the whole point of creating a plan is to alleviate customers’ confusion. Having more than four steps may actually add to, rather than reduce, confusion. The key is to simplify their journey so they are more likely to do business with you.” \- Donald Miller*

The process plan clearly states in 3-4 steps how your process works—and how you will guide your couples to the wedding day they are dreaming of.

**FIRST STEP: What is the first step a couple needs to take to work with you?**

|  |
| :---- |

| ✧ Pro Tip: Common first steps are contacting you, reaching out, filling out your inquiry form, or booking a consultation call with you—but you can also skip to the first step in the process after they are booked, too. |
| :---- |

   
**MIDDLE STEP(S): What step(s) in your process help your ICAs resolve problems, obtain their desires, and set you apart from other photographer’s approaches?**

|  |
| :---- |

   
**FINAL STEP: What is the final step in your process that describes a couple getting what they want by working with you?**

|  |
| :---- |

### **And Calls Them to Action**

*“Most people think they’re overselling when, in truth, their calls to action fall softer than a whisper.” \- Donald Miller*

- Direct call to action: used when we are clearly asking a couple to take or commit to the next step in the process *(i.e. “contact me,” “inquire,” or “request pricing information” on your website… or “would you like me to send over a contract?” while on a consultation call)*  
- Transitional call to action: a softer ask, typically for something with less perceived risk *(i.e. “download this free guide” on your site)*

The most effective call to action you will probably rely on as a photographer is on your website, inviting prospective clients to take the leap from interested to inquiring. 

**Write the clear, direct call to action you want your ICA to encounter on your website:**

|  |
| :---- |

### **That Helps Them Avoid Failure**

*“If there are no stakes, there is no story” \- Donald Miller*

**What would a couple miss out on if they didn’t hire *you*?**

|  |
| :---- |

**Questions and prompts to help you brainstorm:**

* What would your couples lose if they tried to DIY the services you offer?  
* What would happen if they selected a photographer with less experience?  
* What would be the potential downsides to your ICA working with a photographer who didn’t specialize in their type of wedding day, without your expertise?  
* What would happen if they went with a photographer with a less thorough workflow?  
* What would they miss out on if they went with someone who didn’t offer as customized a service as you do?  
* What would be the result if your couples believed some of the myths and misconceptions you wrote down in the “Who Gives Them a Plan” section?  
* What disappointments do you help your couples avoid?  
* Thinking back to your values from the “Identify Your Core Values” section, what outcomes become impossible when you stick to those values?  
* What negative situations have you seen other photographers dealing with that you actively prevent through your services or workflow?

| ✧ Pro Tip: Like problems and empathy, couples won’t realize the *“so what”* of a prospective failure unless it’s truthful.  |
| :---- |

### **And Ends in a Success**

*“The purpose of life is to grow. The nature of life is to change. The challenge of life is to overcome.” \- William Arthur Ward*

**Describe in as much vivid detail as you can how amazing a couple’s wedding experience and photos could be by hiring you—focusing especially on how it *feels*:**

|  |
| :---- |

**Questions and prompts to help you brainstorm:**

* How has working with you changed, transformed, or improved your couples’ lives, planning processes, or wedding day experiences?   
* Where do you envision your brand to be taking your couples?  
* What will it *feel* like for your couple when they are standing there on their wedding day, about to say their vows, looking into their partner’s eyes—having the experience they’ve been longing for?  
* What will it *feel* for your couples when they open up their gallery of images for the first time, to be reliving those moments together and being able to share them with their loved ones?  
* What is the emotional afterstate of you guiding them through their problems?

You can also revisit the wants you have previously identified for your ICAs—because those wants probably correspond to some vision of success:

| ✧ Pro Tip: Still struggling to envision what success looks and feels like for couples working with you? Skip ahead to the “Before and After” exercise immediately following this one—and then circle back to write out the most meaningful definition of success\! |
| :---- |

**Now, summarize in no more than 25 words what “success” you create for your couples:**

|  |
| :---- |

| ✧ Success Statement Examples “a wedding day where you’re fully supported, celebrated, and can be your truest self” “the relaxing and romantic wedding experience you deserve” “an all-inclusive, done-for-you experience where you can just relax and enjoy” “memorable photography and an unforgettable, once-in-a-lifetime experience” |
| :---- |

### **The 7 Elements of Your Brand Story** {#the-7-elements-of-your-brand-story}

This brings together all three elements you have created throughout this section, to build one cohesive brand story.  Start by copying and pasting, and then adjusting your answers until you have your brand story.

| A Character |  |
| :---: | :---- |
| Has a Problem | *Villain*:  *External*:  *Internal*:  *Philosophical: * |
| And Meets a Guide | *Empathy*:   *Authority*:  |
| Who Gives Them a Plan | *First…*  *Then…*  *Finally…*  |
| And Calls Them to Action |  |
| That Helps Them Avoid Failure |  |
| And Ends in a Success |  |

## 

## Before and After Working With You

Let’s create a more in-depth picture of what the *character transformation* could look like when a couple chooses to work with you.

**What does your target market of couples HAVE?** 

When you’re thinking about what they *have* before working with you, consider the problems you’ve previously identified:

* The worst villain my couples face  
* And the biggest external problem my couples face (that I help solve)  


When you’re thinking about what they have after, consider more tangible, defined concepts: peace of mind, reduced workload in the planning process, more time, and the ability to be present. 

| What Your Couples Will *HAVE*… |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| Before Working With You | After Working With You |
|  |  |

**What is your ideal client FEELING?**  

Take into account their feelings about:

* their wedding  
* their images  
* themselves  
* their self-esteem and confidence  
* their aspirational identity and how they want to position themselves in the world

When you’re thinking about what they *feel* before working with you, consider the internal problems, philosophical problems, and fears you’ve already identified: 

* The worst internal problem my couples face (that I can help solve)  
* The biggest philosophical problem my couples face (that my business is positioned against)  
* My ICA’s fears.

When you’re thinking about what they *feel* after, consider if there is anything they might realize or accept about themselves and what it would *feel* like for their wedding day to reach its full potential.

| What Your Couples Will *FEEL*… |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| Before Working With You | After Working With You |
|  |  |

**How will working with you affect the STATUS of your couple in the eyes of others?**

**Questions and prompts to help you brainstorm:**

* What does this couple want their friends and family to be thinking and saying about how they got married or about their wedding photos?  
* What adjectives do they want their friends to associate with their wedding photos *(i.e. “magazine-worthy” or “one-of-a-kind and unique” or “just so them”)*?   
* How will working with you and receiving your images help your ICA be seen in their social circle how they want to be seen?  
* Thinking back to your differentiators, what can they get by working with you that is rare or scarce within the photography industry or beyond?

| What STATUS will your couple hold in the eyes of others… |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| Before Working With You | After Working With You |
|  |  |

**What sense of MEANING will your couple expect or discover in their wedding day?** 

Getting married is a meaning-making process—the couple is intentionally creating a sense of *meaning* for themselves and what their upcoming marriage represents within their wedding day. 

They have never gotten married to each other before… but you have photographed and experienced many wedding days *(or will\!)* as part of your job. Based on your authority and expertise, you can help them uncover meaning in parts of their wedding experience that they never anticipated.

| What Sense of MEANING Your Couples Will Find in Their Wedding Day… |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| Before Working With You | After Working With You |
|  |  |

## 

## Refine Your Unique Value Proposition (UVP)

Now that you have your brand story, you can use it to refine your UVP from the previous video and create a powerful, clear, and concise one-line version of your UVP using your brand story. It should include:

* a focus on the transformation the character undergoes—not you  
* one of your differentiators, likely in the “plan” part  
* clear language that is not ambiguous and does not use terms specific to the photography industry  
* 1 problem at maximum  
* clear, storytelling-oriented language  
* aligned elements that tell a cohesive story about how your plan helps your target market solve their problem and achieve success

**Template 1**

**I help** \[character with a problem\] **by** \[your plan\] **to achieve** \[success\]

I help \[                                                                  \] by \[                                                                \] to achieve \[                                                                  \]

**Template 2**

**I help** \[character who wants something\] **achieve** \[success\] **with** \[your plan\]

I help \[                                                                  \] achieve \[                                                                \] with \[                                                                  \]

**Template 3**

**I help** \[character\] **do/achieve/get** \[what your character wants\] **so they can** \[description of success\] **without** \[problem, fear, challenge, or possible failure\]

I help \[                                                                  \] do/achieve/get   
\[                                                                \] so they can \[                                                                  \]  
without \[                                                                  \]

**Template 4**

**By/with** \[your plan\], **I help** \[character with a problem\] **reach** \[success\]

By/with \[                                                                  \], I help \[                                                                \] reach \[                                                                  \]

**Template 4**

**A** \[your plan\] **for** \[character who wants something\] **to** \[success\]

A \[                                                                  \] for \[                                                                \]   
to \[                                                                  \]

**Place your revised, shortened unique value proposition (UVP) here:** 

|  |
| :---- |

# 

# Brand Persona \- Brand Voice and Personality

## Exploring Your Brand Voice

**What is a brand voice?**

Your brand’s unique personality and presentation—which includes how you talk, write, and engage with others. It should be consistent across all digital platforms and in-person interactions and resonate with your target market and ideal client avatars. 

It might seem obvious, that you simply write and talk like yourself… but taking time to evaluate your brand voice and make some key decisions about what you want your brand voice to sound like can be incredibly helpful to make sure it is in alignment with your ideal client avatars and clearly distinguishable and consistent. It can also help you get better results if you decide to hire any sort of copywriter in the future. 

**Step 1: Research what you’ve already written**

**SOCIAL MEDIA CAPTIONS & BLOG POSTS: Identify the content that has performed best or emotionally resonated with your target market. How would you describe the voice, and are there any trends or similarities in how you phrase things or in the vibe you give off?**

|  |
| :---- |

**REVIEWS & FEEDBACK: Read your reviews and feedback again. Are there any adjectives or common themes used to describe your brand voice or personality?**

|  |
| :---- |

**Step 2: Answer self-reflective questions**

If you’re not sure what your brand voice is, read through the questions below and answer these self-reflective questions to help you identify your brand voice characteristics below. You don’t have to answer them all if you aren’t sure about the answers—just try to do as many as you can to establish a strong foundation for your brand voice. 

**Using just single words, what emotions and feelings do you want your clients to have after reading an email from you?**

|  |
| :---- |

**What do you try to avoid when communicating with others?**

|  |
| :---- |

**How would you describe your “tone” or “vibe” when communicating with clients?**

|  |
| :---- |

**What are three words your partner or best friend would use to describe you?**

|  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |

**What words or phrases do you find yourself using more than others?**

|  |
| :---- |

**What commonly used words or phrases in the wedding industry do you personally hate and would never use?**

|  |
| :---- |

**What commonly used words or phrases in the photography industry do you personally hate and would never use?**

|  |
| :---- |

**How do you greet your clients in your emails?**

|  |
| :---- |

**How do you sign off from your emails?**

|  |
| :---- |

**How would your target market describe themselves using only single words or short phrases?**

|  |
| :---- |

   
**How would you describe the types of unconventional weddings or elopements you want to photograph using only single words or short phrases?**

|  |
| :---- |

## Brand Voice Characteristics

| Consistent Descriptions *repetition can help people associate specific words with you and your business—so determine the words or phrases you want to use to most commonly describe…*  |  |
| ----- | :---- |
| The experience you create for your couples |  |
| How a couple will *feel* while working with you |  |
| How the wedding days your ICAs are planning *should* be like |  |
| Other description notes |  |

 

| Vocabulary *establishing what words and phrases you want to most commonly use in your brand voice* |  |
| ----- | :---- |
| Verbs *(action words like: photographing, grow, enjoy)* |  |
| Adjectives *(describing words like: beautiful, breathtaking, special)* |  |
| Nouns *(words associated with people, places, and things—like: photos, couple, moment, rings)* |  |
| Interesting, unusual, or meaningful phrases |  |
| What words or phrases do you always want to avoid? |  |
| Are you comfortable with cursing? |  |
| Do you use slang—and, if so, what slang? |  |
| Other vocabulary notes |  |

 

| Tone *highlight where you are at on these various tonal spectrums* |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| ----- | ----- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---- |
| Funny | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | Serious |
| Formal | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | Casual |
| Enthusiastic  | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | Matter-of-Fact |
| Personal | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | Professional |
| Spontaneous | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | Planned |
| Modern | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | Traditional |
| Verbose | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | Concise |
| Other tone notes |  |  |  |  |  |  |

| Grammar and Punctuation *setting clear guidelines for how you want to use grammar and punctuation* |  |
| ----- | :---- |
| How often do you use exclamation marks? |  |
| How often do you ask questions? |  |
| How do you separate two parts of a sentence  *(and, &, hyphen, em-dash, semicolon, etc.)*?  |  |
| Do you use numerals or spell out numbers *(i.e. “four” or “4”)*? |  |
| Do you always use full sentences or are you okay with sentence fragments? |  |
| Is there anything you spell differently than usual? |  |
| Other grammar or punctuation notes |  |

| Formatting *to ensure your content visually looks similar across platforms* |  |
| ----- | :---- |
| How do you add emphasis to important words or phrases  *(bold, italics, all caps)*?  |  |
| Do you use emojis? If so, list your 10 most frequently used emojis and how you use them |  |
| Other formatting  notes |  |

| Defining Limits *to provide additional clarification for what your business is—and is not* |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| I am… *(i.e. I am… “refined”)* | But I am not… *(i.e. But I am not “stuffy or dull”)* |
|  |  |
|  |  |

| “Do” & “Do Not”s *to clarify any additional recommendations or what you would like to discourage with your brand voice* |  |
| ----- | :---- |
| Do |  |
| Do Not |  |

| Miscellaneous *the final touches of your brand voice* |  |
| ----- | :---- |
| When referring to your business, do you use first person *(“I” or “we”)* or third person *(“they,” “she,” or your business name)*? |  |
| Does your business ever use humor? If so, what kind(s)?  |  |
| Other miscellaneous notes |  |

| Overview *finalize how you want your brand voice to sound and be positioned in just five words* |  |  |  |  |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Word 1** | **Word 2** | **Word 3** | **Word 4** | **Word 5** |
|  |  |  |  |  |

**Final review:** 

Does your brand voice as established through these characteristics…

- [ ] Align with your company values?  
- [ ] Take into consideration your ideal client avatars? 

# 

# Brand Persona \- Portfolio & Visual Identity

## Create Your Brand Guide Template

Now, it is time to pull together the most important information from this template into a simplified, cohesive brand guide template. 

The benefit of doing this is that you have a document you can quickly reference whenever you are creating content to make sure you are in alignment with everything you have established throughout this fillable template…

And you can share it with any future independent contractors you work with or employees you hire to establish consistency without having to personally instruct them on everything you’ve decided here.

Don’t wait to do this until you’re ready to outsource or need a concise reminder of what your brand is and stands for—take a few minutes to do it now while you still have all of these thoughts and decisions at the top of your mind\!

In case you missed it earlier, here is a link to a template you can use to create your [**brand guide**](https://www.canva.com/design/DAFp-S4uoxU/JRPHMHSXtcYgSs1-NVxu7A/view?utm_content=DAFp-S4uoxU&utm_campaign=designshare&utm_medium=link&utm_source=publishsharelink&mode=preview).

## 

## Guiding Questions

**What is a brand visual identity?**

It is the outward expression of your brand—including your brand name, visual appearance, logo, color palette, typography, illustrations, iconography, photography portfolio, and so forth. You want it to be:

1. Differentiated  
2. Flexible  
3. Cohesive  
4. Aligned with the non-visual elements of your brand *(or everything that has been established throughout this fillable template)*

**What are the key brand traits you want to express through your visuals?**

|   |
| :---- |

**What do you want people to feel when they see your brand?**

|   |
| :---- |

## 

## 5 Essentials For Your Visual Brand

1. **Your brand logo or wordmark**

Your brand’s unique signifier is your identity in its simplest form. It should immediately call to mind your organization — and (as importantly) call to heart the distinct emotional appeal at your brand’s core. However simple they appear, logos are painstakingly thought out and strategically designed, refined and revised many times in the design process. 

2. **Brand color palette**

When choosing your color palette, think carefully what emotions you want to evoke, what audiences you’re addressing and all your needs: website and online social presence; corporate, employee and investor communications; reports, brochures, sales tools and presentations as well as print materials.

3. **Typefaces**

When choosing your color palette, think carefully what emotions you want to evoke, what audiences you’re addressing and all your needs: website and online social presence; corporate, employee and investor communications; reports, brochures, sales tools and presentations as well as print materials.

4. **Standard typographic treatments**

Your typographic identity should include ways of handling key types of text, especially your tagline or your address. Developing a consistent way of writing and styling headlines or pull- out text really begins to create a distinct voice for your brand. Work to make these similar from one application to the next. 

5. **Consistent style for images**

It’s crucial to use photos that have the same look and feel, no matter what it is. Light and airy, moody or bold/colorful are just a few examples. As long as they look similar and are used consistently, you are in good shape. You can use stock photos, but I really consider investing in some brand photos, so that you have a collection of images unique to you.

## 

## Types of Logos

![][image6]

## Design Your Logo

When it comes to good logo design, there are a few simple, but essential rules.

| IS IT APPROPRIATE? | IS IT SIMPLE? | IS IT VERSATILE? | IS IT MEMORABLE? |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Does it reflect your brand’s personality? Will it appeal to our target audience? A logo must always be appropriate and representative of the brand behind it.  |  A simpler logo increases recognition and makes it easier to implement across all platforms. A poorly designed logo will make you look un- professional.  | Versatility is one of the most essential quality of a good logo design. In the 21st cen- tury, brands are highly visible. They are exposed to a wide range of media. |  The memorability of a logo of- ten goes back to its simplicity and uniqueness. Just ask yourself: Does it stick in your head after seeing it only a few times?  |

| Use the space below to sketch a few logo variations: |
| ----- |
|  |

## Color Psychology

Colors and emotions are closely linked. Warm colors can evoke different emotions than cool & bright colors can create different feelings than muted.

![][image7]

|   |  Why is Color  so Important? |
| ----- | ----- |
|  | Color is a powerful communication tool and can be used to signal action, influence mood, and even influence physiological reactions. Certain colors have been associated with increased blood pressure, increased metabolism and eyestrain.  |

## Color Inspiration

Colors tell the story of your business. They can both attract and repel your ideal client avatar and they’re a crucial component of any brand.

![][image8]

## Brand Imagery

The purpose of brand imagery is to communicate the right messages with your target audience to develop strong emotions when they encounter your brand.

| Photography There are many styles and subsets of photography to think about and a multitude of ways to create or source your photos if that is what you want to use to present your brand image.  |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Illustration Illustration is another widely used visual tactic. The beauty of utilizing illustration is that you are only limited by your imagination (and your budget).  |  |
| Infographic An infographic tells a story within itself and can be very powerful at distilling and communicating complex ideas, products or processes.  |  |
| Icons & Symbols Icons and symbols are a great way to tell a story with limited words. Nopt as primary image source, but rather a detailed aspect of a process or product within the brand. |  |

|  To create Brand Imagery \- be consistent | With that said, don’t forget about consistency. Mixing up playful illustrations with com- plex infographics and formal photographs will not help your business be memorable. Pick a style of imagery that fits to your brand and go on with it. |
| :---: | :---- |
